note: Peer-reviewed Nature 2026 version of 'The AI Scientist' (Lu et al., Nature 651:914-919, DOI 10.1038/s41586-026-10265-5). All 6 citing claims adjudicated against the actual PDF text + Table 1. 5/6 ACCURATE, 1 IMPRECISE-OK (C014). Critical NUM claim C016 (0.69) verified against Table 1. No factual errors requiring a fix; three low-severity reverse-sweep notes on conflation/wording provenance/comparative scope.

C012.evidence: Grouped background citation: 'AI Scientist systems ... have proliferated rapidly'. lu2026aiscientist IS The AI Scientist, the canonical end-to-end automated-research system, and the paper's own intro documents the proliferation of component systems (ideation, lit-review, coding agents) and positions itself as the first full-pipeline instance ('a system that autonomously navigates the entire research life cycle ... has remained out of reach until now', p.914). Inclusion in a list of AI Scientist systems is apt.

C013.evidence: Label 'closed-loop manuscript-writing pipeline' fairly describes this system: 'The AI Scientist ... creates research ideas, writes code, runs experiments, plots and analyses data, writes the entire scientific manuscript, and performs its own peer review' (Abstract, p.914). The loop is closed by the system reviewing its own output ('the paper generated by The AI Scientist undergoes a review by The Automated Reviewer', p.915). Co-citation with lu2024aiscientist (preprint) is appropriate.

C014.evidence: Claim: prior work (incl. lu2026aiscientist) 'provides qualitative characterizations of individual systems but no shared framework'. Load-bearing part (no shared cross-system evaluation framework) is TRUE: the paper describes a single system (The AI Scientist) and its bespoke internal evaluator; it does not abstract a reusable framework for classifying/comparing the class of AI-scientist systems. Minor tension: the word 'qualitative' undersells the paper's substantial QUANTITATIVE self-evaluation (Table 1 reviewer metrics; Fig 1b/3c compute+model scaling with P-values). Acceptable for a grouped background citation; the 'no shared framework' assertion holds.
C014.fix: Optional only: if any reviewer objects to 'qualitative', note that lu2026aiscientist includes quantitative internal evaluation but still offers no cross-system framework. No change required.

C015.evidence: All three attributed layers are real in the paper. V_e (replication nodes, mean±s.d.): 'Replication nodes execute replicates of their parent experiments using different random seeds. Typically, several replication nodes are created to enable the calculation of statistical measures (mean and s.d.) of experimental outcomes' + 'The script produces figures that explicitly show mean and s.d.' (Methods, p.919). V_s (automated reviewer): 'The Automated Reviewer ... automatically evaluates the scientific quality' (p.915); full Methods 'The Automated Reviewer' section p.919. V_h (actual peer review via ICLR workshop): 'We submitted three generated manuscripts to the formal peer-review process of a workshop at a top-tier machine learning conference ... the ICLR 2025 ... (ICBINB) workshop. This was the only venue that we submitted to.' (p.916). Venue = ICLR (ICBINB workshop @ ICLR 2025) is CORRECT, not the wrong venue.

C016.evidence: Table 1 (p.915) reports Automated Reviewer 'Balanced accuracy' = 0.69 ± 0.04 on the pre-cutoff (2017-2024) dataset. Corroborated in Methods Validation: 'The Automated Reviewer achieved a comparable balanced accuracy with humans (69% versus 66%)' (p.919), where 69% = automated reviewer. So the paper DOES report the automated reviewer's balanced accuracy as 0.69, and 0.69 is specifically the balanced-accuracy metric (not AUC, though AUC is coincidentally also 0.69±0.09). Claim number is exact. CAVEAT (see reverse): 0.69 is the pre-cutoff/contamination-susceptible value; the 'clean' post-cutoff (2025) balanced accuracy is 0.66±0.03.

C017.evidence: Manuscript's characterization ('the biased V_s reviewer stays uncalibrated') is an editorial inference that is GROUNDED in the paper's own data, though the paper never uses the words 'biased' or 'uncalibrated' for the reviewer (the token 'bias' appears in the paper ONLY as 'colour-biased MNIST dataset', an experiment example, p.917-fig). Support for accept-bias/miscalibration: Table 1 (p.915) Automated Reviewer FPR = 0.45±0.10 (pre-cutoff) and 0.52±0.10 (post-cutoff) versus Human FPR = 0.17 — i.e., the automated reviewer wrongly accepts ~half of true-reject papers, far more lenient than humans. Contamination self-admission: 'data contamination may exist, as balanced decision accuracy decreases from 69% before to 66% in the year after the cutoff' (p.916). Both substantiate 'biased/uncalibrated' as a fair, data-backed critique. It is the manuscript's own analytical judgment, NOT a false claim attributed to the paper.
C017.fix: Optional strengthening: cite Table 1 FPR (0.45-0.52 vs human 0.17) as the concrete basis for 'biased/uncalibrated', since the paper does not state those words narratively. Not required for accuracy.

reverse_findings.fact: TWO distinct 0.69/0.66 comparisons exist and are easy to conflate. (a) Automated reviewer balanced accuracy 0.69 (pre-cutoff 2017-2024) vs 0.66 (post-cutoff 2025 'clean') — the contamination test. (b) Automated reviewer 0.69 vs Human 0.66 — the human-comparison (Methods Validation '69% versus 66%'; Table 1 Human BA = 0.66). The manuscript's C016 cites 0.69, which is legitimately the automated reviewer's balanced accuracy, but is the pre-cutoff value; the coincidence that human BA (0.66) equals the post-cutoff automated BA (0.66) makes misreading likely.
reverse_findings.loc: Table 1 (p.915); contamination para p.916; Methods Validation p.919
reverse_findings.mismatch: Citing 0.69 (pre-cutoff, potentially contamination-inflated) rather than the 0.66 'clean' post-cutoff value is internally slightly generous given C017 simultaneously calls the reviewer biased/uncalibrated. Number is accurate but the choice of the higher figure is worth an internal-consistency check.

reverse_findings.fact: Strongest paper-internal evidence for C017's 'biased/uncalibrated' is the FPR gap: Automated Reviewer FPR 0.45 (pre) / 0.52 (post) vs Human FPR 0.17 (Table 1). The paper presents this only as tabular data — it does NOT narrate the reviewer as biased or perform a calibration analysis.
reverse_findings.mismatch: C017's 'uncalibrated' is the manuscript's inference; the paper offers no explicit calibration claim. Well-supported by FPR data but the exact words are not in the source — provenance should be attributed to Table 1, not to the paper's prose.

reverse_findings.fact: 'giving the most layers in the class' (C015) is a cross-system comparative superlative about the manuscript's own taxonomy, not a statement made by or about lu2026aiscientist alone.
reverse_findings.loc: N/A within lu2026aiscientist (comparative claim spans the manuscript's whole system set)
reverse_findings.mismatch: The three layers attributed to THIS paper (V_e/V_s/V_h) are all real and correctly sourced, so the citation is sound; but 'most layers in the class' cannot be verified from this single paper — its correctness depends on the manuscript's tally across all cited systems, which is out of scope for this citation audit.
